intro_text: Hi there! 😊 Today we explore Circles — one of the most beautiful shapes in mathematics! From chords to arcs, from angles to cyclic quadrilaterals — every theorem in this chapter is elegant and powerful! 🎯
complete_message: You have mastered Circles! Every point, chord and arc now makes perfect sense to you 💪

explain_questions.question: What are the basic terms related to a circle?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What are the basic terms related to a circle?

steps.t: A CIRCLE is the collection of all points in a plane equidistant from a fixed point called the CENTRE. The fixed distance is the RADIUS. DIAMETER = 2 × Radius (longest chord, passes through centre).
steps.s: A circle is all points at equal distance from the centre. Radius is that distance. Diameter is twice the radius.

steps.t: CHORD: a line segment joining any two points on the circle. DIAMETER is the longest chord. ARC: a piece of the circle between two points — MINOR ARC (smaller) and MAJOR ARC (larger). SEMICIRCLE: half the circle.
steps.s: A chord joins two points on the circle. An arc is a piece of the circle. Minor arc is smaller, major arc is larger.

steps.t: SECTOR: region between two radii and an arc (like a pizza slice!). SEGMENT: region between a chord and an arc — MINOR SEGMENT and MAJOR SEGMENT. SECANT: a line that cuts the circle at two points. TANGENT: touches at exactly one point.
steps.s: Sector is like a pizza slice between two radii. Segment is between a chord and an arc. Tangent touches the circle at one point only.

explain_questions.confirm_question: What is the longest chord of a circle called?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: What is the longest chord of a circle called?
nudges: This chord passes through the centre of the circle
It is equal to twice the radius
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="ci1s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Circle: all points equidistant from centre | Radius=distance</text></g><g id="ci1s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Chord: 2 pts on circle | Diameter=longest chord=2r</text></g><g id="ci1s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Sector=pizza slice | Segment=chord+arc | Tangent=1 touch pt</text></g><g id="ci1ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Diameter = longest chord</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What does it mean for a chord to subtend an angle?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What does it mean for a chord to subtend an angle?

steps.t: When a chord PQ is drawn in a circle with centre O, it forms angle POQ at the centre — this is the angle SUBTENDED by chord PQ at the centre O. The word 'subtend' means 'to be opposite to'.
steps.s: A chord subtends an angle at the centre by joining its endpoints to the centre. The angle POQ is subtended by chord PQ at centre O.

steps.t: Theorem 9.1: Equal chords of a circle subtend EQUAL angles at the centre. If AB = CD (both chords), then angle AOB = angle COD. Proof: triangles AOB and COD are congruent by SSS (all radii equal, AB=CD).
steps.s: Equal chords make equal angles at the centre. Proved using SSS congruence since all radii are equal.

steps.t: Converse (Theorem 9.2): If angles subtended by chords at the centre are equal, then the chords are equal. Bigger chord → bigger angle at centre. Smaller chord → smaller angle. They go hand in hand!
steps.s: If angles at centre are equal, the chords are equal. Bigger chord means bigger angle. They are directly related.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Equal chords of a circle subtend ______ angles at the centre.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Equal chords of a circle subtend blank angles at the centre.
nudges: This is Theorem 9.1 — a fundamental circle theorem
If AB=CD (chords), then angle AOB = angle COD
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="ci2s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Chord PQ subtends ∠POQ at centre O</text></g><g id="ci2s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Theorem 9.1: AB=CD → ∠AOB=∠COD (by SSS)</text></g><g id="ci2s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Converse: ∠AOB=∠COD → AB=CD</text></g><g id="ci2ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Equal chords → Equal angles at centre</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is the perpendicular from centre to a chord theorem?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is the perpendicular from the centre to a chord theorem?

steps.t: Theorem 9.3: The perpendicular from the centre of a circle to a chord BISECTS the chord. If OM is perpendicular to chord AB, then AM = MB. The perpendicular drops exactly at the midpoint of the chord!
steps.s: Perpendicular from centre to chord bisects the chord. OM perpendicular to AB means AM equals MB.

steps.t: Proof: Join OA and OB (both radii, so OA=OB). Triangles OAM and OBM are congruent by RHS (right angle, same hypotenuse OA=OB, common side OM). So AM = MB by CPCT.
steps.s: Join radii OA and OB. The two triangles are congruent by RHS. Therefore AM equals MB by CPCT.

steps.t: Converse: The line joining the centre to the midpoint of a chord is PERPENDICULAR to the chord. So if M is the midpoint of chord AB, then OM is perpendicular to AB. Both theorem and converse are used in CBSE proofs!
steps.s: Converse: the line from centre to midpoint of chord is perpendicular to the chord. Both directions are important.

explain_questions.confirm_question: If OM is perpendicular to chord AB and AM = 5 cm, what is AB?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: If OM is perpendicular to chord AB and AM equals 5 cm, what is AB?
nudges: Perpendicular from centre bisects the chord
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="ci3s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Theorem 9.3: OM ⊥ AB → AM = MB (bisects chord)</text></g><g id="ci3s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Proof: OA=OB (radii), OM common → RHS → CPCT: AM=MB</text></g><g id="ci3s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Converse: M is midpoint of AB → OM ⊥ AB</text></g><g id="ci3ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">MB = AM = 5 → AB = 10 cm</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is the unique circle through three non-collinear points?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is the unique circle theorem for three non-collinear points?

steps.t: Theorem: There is ONE and ONLY ONE circle passing through three given non-collinear points. Through 1 point: infinite circles possible. Through 2 points: infinite circles. Through 3 non-collinear points: EXACTLY ONE circle!
steps.s: One point allows infinite circles. Two points allow infinite circles. Three non-collinear points give exactly one unique circle.

steps.t: If the three points are COLLINEAR (on the same line), NO circle can pass through all three — a straight line is not part of any circle! This is why we need 'non-collinear' for the theorem to work.
steps.s: If the three points are collinear, no circle passes through all three. The points must be non-collinear.

steps.t: Method to find the circle: draw perpendicular bisectors of the line segments joining consecutive points. They all meet at the CIRCUMCENTER — the centre of the unique circle through the three points!
steps.s: The circumcenter is where the perpendicular bisectors of the sides of the triangle formed meet. That is the centre of the circle.

explain_questions.confirm_question: How many circles can pass through three non-collinear points?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: How many circles can pass through three non-collinear points?
nudges: Three non-collinear points form a unique triangle
Every triangle has exactly one circumscribed circle
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="ci4s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">1 point → ∞ circles | 2 points → ∞ circles</text></g><g id="ci4s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">3 non-collinear points → EXACTLY 1 circle!</text></g><g id="ci4s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">3 collinear points → NO circle possible</text></g><g id="ci4ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Exactly ONE circle through 3 non-collinear points</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is the equal chords and distance from centre theorem?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is the equal chords and distance from centre theorem?

steps.t: Distance of a chord from the centre = length of the perpendicular from centre to chord. Theorem 9.6: Equal chords of a circle are EQUIDISTANT from the centre. If AB = CD, then their perpendicular distances from centre O are equal.
steps.s: Equal chords are at equal distances from the centre. Distance means the perpendicular from the centre to the chord.

steps.t: Converse: Chords equidistant from the centre are equal in length. So if OM = ON (perpendicular distances), then chord AB = chord CD. The theorem and its converse are both important!
steps.s: Converse: chords at equal distances from centre are equal in length.

steps.t: Key insight: The CLOSER a chord is to the centre, the LONGER it is! The diameter (passing through centre, distance=0) is the longest chord. As a chord moves away from the centre, it gets shorter.
steps.s: Closer to centre means longer chord. Diameter has distance zero and is the longest. Chords farther from centre are shorter.

explain_questions.confirm_question: Equal chords of a circle are ______ from the centre.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Equal chords of a circle are blank from the centre.
nudges: If AB = CD (equal chords), their perpendicular distances from O are...
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="ci5s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Distance = perpendicular from centre to chord</text></g><g id="ci5s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Theorem 9.6: AB=CD → OM=ON (equidistant)</text></g><g id="ci5s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Closer to centre → LONGER chord | Diameter = closest (dist=0)</text></g><g id="ci5ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">Equal chords are equidistant from centre</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is the angle subtended by an arc at the centre theorem?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is the angle subtended by an arc at the centre theorem?

steps.t: Theorem 9.8 (Most important circle theorem!): The angle subtended by an arc at the CENTRE is DOUBLE the angle subtended by the same arc at any point on the remaining part of the circle.
steps.s: The central angle is double the inscribed angle subtended by the same arc. This is the most important theorem.

steps.t: Example: Arc PQ subtends angle POQ = 100° at centre O. Then at any point R on the major arc, angle PRQ = 50° (half of 100°). The inscribed angle is always HALF the central angle!
steps.s: If central angle is 100 degrees, inscribed angle is 50 degrees. The inscribed angle is always half the central angle.

steps.t: Special case: If the arc is a SEMICIRCLE (diameter), central angle = 180°. So inscribed angle = 90°! Theorem: Angle in a semicircle is always a RIGHT ANGLE. This is used very frequently!
steps.s: Angle in a semicircle is 90 degrees. If the arc is a semicircle, the inscribed angle is always 90 degrees.

explain_questions.confirm_question: If the angle subtended by an arc at the centre is 80°, what is the angle at the circumference?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: If the central angle is 80 degrees, what is the inscribed angle at the circumference?
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="ci6s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Theorem 9.8: Central angle = 2 × Inscribed angle</text></g><g id="ci6s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">∠POQ=100° at centre → ∠PRQ=50° at circumference</text></g><g id="ci6s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Semicircle: central=180° → inscribed=90° (right angle!)</text></g><g id="ci6ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">80 ÷ 2 = 40°</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What are angles in the same segment theorem?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What are angles in the same segment theorem?

steps.t: Theorem 9.9: Angles in the SAME SEGMENT of a circle are EQUAL. If P and Q are two points on the major arc of chord AB, then angle APB = angle AQB. All inscribed angles in the same segment are equal!
steps.s: All angles inscribed in the same segment of a circle are equal. Angle APB equals angle AQB when P and Q are on the same arc.

steps.t: This means: any number of points on the major arc — the angle subtended by chord AB at ALL of them is exactly the same! This is a beautiful result — no matter where you stand on the arc, the view angle is constant.
steps.s: No matter where on the same arc you stand, the angle you see the chord at is always the same.

steps.t: Converse (Theorem 9.10): If a line segment AB subtends equal angles at two points P and Q on the same side of AB, then A, B, P, Q are concyclic (they lie on a common circle). Four concyclic points!
steps.s: Converse: if equal angles are subtended at two points on the same side, the four points lie on a circle.

explain_questions.confirm_question: If angles in the same segment are always equal, and angle APB = 55°, what is angle AQB?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: Angles APB and AQB are in the same segment. Angle APB is 55 degrees. What is angle AQB?
nudges: Theorem 9.9: angles in same segment are equal
P and Q are both on the same arc
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="ci7s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Theorem 9.9: Angles in same segment are EQUAL</text></g><g id="ci7s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">∠APB = ∠AQB (both on major arc of chord AB)</text></g><g id="ci7s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Converse: equal angles on same side → 4 concyclic points</text></g><g id="ci7ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">∠AQB = ∠APB = 55°</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is a cyclic quadrilateral and what are its properties?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is a cyclic quadrilateral and what are its properties?

steps.t: A CYCLIC QUADRILATERAL is a quadrilateral whose ALL FOUR VERTICES lie on a circle (the circumscribed circle). Examples: rectangle, square, isosceles trapezium — all can be cyclic!
steps.s: A cyclic quadrilateral has all four vertices on a circle. Rectangle, square, and isosceles trapezium are cyclic.

steps.t: Theorem 9.11: The sum of EITHER PAIR of opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is 180° (supplementary)! In cyclic quadrilateral ABCD: angle A + angle C = 180° AND angle B + angle D = 180°.
steps.s: In a cyclic quadrilateral, opposite angles are supplementary — they add up to 180 degrees.

steps.t: Converse (Theorem 9.12): If the sum of a pair of opposite angles of a quadrilateral is 180°, then the quadrilateral is cyclic. So to prove a quad is cyclic, just show one pair of opposite angles sums to 180°!
steps.s: Converse: if one pair of opposite angles sums to 180 degrees, the quadrilateral is cyclic.

explain_questions.confirm_question: In cyclic quadrilateral ABCD, angle A = 110°. Find angle C.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: In cyclic quadrilateral ABCD, angle A equals 110 degrees. Find angle C.
nudges: Opposite angles of cyclic quadrilateral add to 180°
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="ci8s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Cyclic quad: all 4 vertices on a circle</text></g><g id="ci8s1" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="46" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="66" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Theorem 9.11: ∠A+∠C = 180° and ∠B+∠D = 180°</text></g><g id="ci8s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Converse: opp. angles sum 180° → cyclic quad</text></g><g id="ci8ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">∠C = 180° − 110° = 70°</text></g>

explain_questions.question: What is the angle in a semicircle?
explain_questions.question_spoken: What is the angle in a semicircle?

steps.t: THEOREM: The angle subtended by a diameter at any point on the circle is always 90° (a right angle). In other words, the angle in a semicircle is always a right angle!
steps.s: Angle in a semicircle is 90 degrees. A diameter subtends a right angle at any point on the circle.

steps.t: Why? The diameter creates a central angle of 180°. By Theorem 9.8, the inscribed angle = 180° ÷ 2 = 90°. Clean and elegant! This result is also called Thales' Theorem.
steps.s: Diameter creates 180 degree central angle. Half of 180 is 90. So inscribed angle is always 90 degrees. This is Thales Theorem.

steps.t: Application: If AB is a diameter and C is any point on the circle (not A or B), then angle ACB = 90°. This is how we prove right angles in circle problems — look for a diameter!
steps.s: If AB is a diameter and C is on the circle, angle ACB is 90 degrees. Look for diameters to identify right angles.

explain_questions.confirm_question: AB is a diameter of a circle. C is any point on the circle. What is angle ACB?
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: AB is a diameter and C is any point on the circle. What is angle ACB?
nudges: This is Thales' Theorem — angle in a semicircle
The diameter subtends a right angle at the circumference
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="ci9s0" opacity="0"><rect x="10" y="8" width="390" height="30" rx="7" fill="#f5e6c8"/><text x="210" y="28" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#d4870a">Angle in semicircle = 90° (Thales Theorem)</text></g><g id="ci9s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="68" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Diameter: central=180° → inscribed = 90° (half)</text></g><g id="ci9s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="108" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">∠ACB = 90° for ANY point C on circle (AB=diameter)</text></g><g id="ci9ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">∠ACB = 90° always</text></g>

explain_questions.question: How do you solve angle problems in circles step by step?
explain_questions.question_spoken: How do you solve angle problems in circles step by step?

steps.t: Step 1: Identify what is given — central angle, inscribed angle, chord, arc, diameter, or cyclic quadrilateral. Step 2: Select the right theorem. Central angle = 2 × inscribed? Same segment? Semicircle? Cyclic quad?
steps.s: Identify what is given. Then select the correct theorem to apply.

steps.t: Example: In a circle, central angle AOB = 120°. Point C on major arc. Find angle ACB. Solution: By Theorem 9.8, angle ACB = ½ × 120° = 60°.
steps.s: Central angle 120 degrees. Inscribed angle equals half, so 60 degrees. Direct application of Theorem 9.8.

steps.t: Common exam types: (1) Find inscribed angle given central angle. (2) Find missing angle in cyclic quadrilateral. (3) Prove angle = 90° using diameter. (4) Use equal chords/arcs. Practice all four types!
steps.s: Four common exam types: inscribed angle from central, cyclic quad angles, right angle using diameter, equal chords and arcs.

explain_questions.confirm_question: If angle BOC = 70° (centre angle) and A is on major arc, find angle BAC.
explain_questions.confirm_question_spoken: If the central angle BOC is 70 degrees and A is on the major arc, find angle BAC.
nudges: Inscribed angle BAC = central angle BOC ÷ 2
explain_questions.anim_svg: <g id="ci10s0" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="30" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">Step 1: Identify given | Step 2: Pick theorem</text></g><g id="ci10s1" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="70" font-size="12" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">∠AOB=120° → ∠ACB=60° (inscribed = half central)</text></g><g id="ci10s2" opacity="0"><text x="22" y="110" font-size="11" font-weight="800" fill="#5a4a30">4 types: inscribed, cyclic quad, semicircle, equal chords</text></g><g id="ci10ans" opacity="0"><rect x="40" y="148" width="320" height="24" rx="7" fill="#eef2eb" stroke="#6b4c2a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="210" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Share Tech Mono" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#7a8c6e">∠BAC = 70 ÷ 2 = 35°</text></g>

practice_questions.question: The longest chord of a circle is called the ______.
practice_questions.question_spoken: The longest chord of a circle is called the blank.
steps: A chord is a line segment joining two points on the circle
The longest possible chord must pass through the centre
This chord is the diameter = 2 × radius
practice_questions.trick: Diameter passes through the CENTRE. Any other chord is shorter. Diameter = longest chord, always!
practice_questions.alt: The farther apart two points on a circle can be, the longer the chord. The maximum distance is across the centre — that is the diameter.

practice_questions.question: Equal chords of a circle subtend ______ angles at the centre.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Equal chords of a circle subtend blank angles at the centre.
practice_questions.trick: Equal chords, equal angles. Bigger chord, bigger angle. Direct relationship between chord length and central angle!
practice_questions.alt: Join chord endpoints to centre forming triangles. If chords are equal and radii are equal (SSS), the triangles are congruent, so central angles are equal.

practice_questions.question: The perpendicular from the centre of a circle to a chord ______ the chord.
practice_questions.question_spoken: The perpendicular from the centre of a circle to a chord blank the chord.
steps: The perpendicular from centre BISECTS the chord
practice_questions.trick: Centre-to-chord perpendicular = bisector. This is the shortest path from centre to chord, and it always hits the midpoint!
practice_questions.alt: Join radii to chord endpoints. Two right triangles formed are congruent by RHS. So the two halves of the chord are equal — the chord is bisected.

practice_questions.question: If OM ⊥ AB (chord) and OM = 6 cm, OA = 10 cm, find AB.
practice_questions.question_spoken: OM is perpendicular to chord AB, OM equals 6 cm, and OA equals 10 cm. Find AB.
steps: By Pythagoras in right triangle OAM: AM² = OA² − OM² = 100 − 36 = 64
AM = 8 cm. Since OM bisects AB: AB = 2 × AM = 16 cm
practice_questions.trick: Use Pythagoras: AM² = OA² − OM². Then double AM to get AB (since perpendicular from centre bisects chord).
practice_questions.alt: Right triangle OAM: hypotenuse OA=10, one leg OM=6. Other leg AM = √(10²−6²) = √(100−36) = √64 = 8. AB = 2×8 = 16.

practice_questions.question: The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is ______ the angle subtended at any point on the remaining circle.
practice_questions.question_spoken: The angle subtended by an arc at the centre is blank the angle at any point on the remaining circle.
steps: This is Theorem 9.8 — the most important circle theorem
So central angle is DOUBLE the inscribed angle
practice_questions.trick: Centre angle = DOUBLE circumference angle. Half it to get inscribed. Double it to get central. This theorem appears in every exam!
practice_questions.alt: Think of it as: the centre has the 'full view' — it sees the arc at twice the angle that any point on the circle sees it.

practice_questions.question: If central angle AOB = 140°, find the inscribed angle ACB where C is on the major arc.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Central angle AOB is 140 degrees. Find inscribed angle ACB where C is on the major arc.
steps: By Theorem 9.8: central angle = 2 × inscribed angle
practice_questions.trick: Inscribed angle = central angle ÷ 2. Always halve the central angle to get the inscribed angle!
practice_questions.alt: Central is double the inscribed. So inscribed is half the central. 140 ÷ 2 = 70°.

practice_questions.question: Angle in a semicircle is always ____°.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Angle in a semicircle is always blank degrees.
steps: Angle in a semicircle is ALWAYS 90° (right angle)
practice_questions.trick: Semicircle → right angle. This is Thales' Theorem. If you see a diameter with a point on the circle, the angle is 90°. No calculation needed!
practice_questions.alt: The arc is a semicircle (180°). The inscribed angle subtends this arc. It must be 180÷2 = 90°.

practice_questions.question: Angles in the same segment of a circle are ______.
practice_questions.question_spoken: Angles in the same segment of a circle are blank.
steps: All inscribed angles subtending the same arc are equal
No matter where on the same segment you are, the angle is the same
practice_questions.trick: Same segment = same angle. All points on the major arc see chord AB at exactly the same angle. Beautiful theorem!
practice_questions.alt: Both angles subtend the same arc. By Theorem 9.8, both equal half the central angle for that arc. Same half → same angle.

practice_questions.question: In a cyclic quadrilateral ABCD, angle A = 85° and angle C = ?
practice_questions.question_spoken: In a cyclic quadrilateral ABCD, angle A equals 85 degrees. Find angle C.
steps: Theorem 9.11: opposite angles of cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°
practice_questions.trick: Cyclic quad: opposite angles are supplementary (add to 180°). One given, subtract from 180 to get the other. Simple!
practice_questions.alt: Opposite means across the quadrilateral. Angle A and C are opposite. They must add to 180°. 180−85=95°.

practice_questions.question: In cyclic quadrilateral PQRS, angle P = 70° and angle Q = 110°. Find angles R and S.
practice_questions.question_spoken: In cyclic quadrilateral PQRS, angle P is 70 and angle Q is 110 degrees. Find angles R and S.
practice_questions.trick: Find opposite pairs. Subtract each from 180. Check that all four sum to 360°. Systematic approach!
practice_questions.alt: P and R are opposite: R = 180−70 = 110. Q and S are opposite: S = 180−110 = 70. Verify: 70+110+110+70=360 ✓

practice_questions.question: How many circles can be drawn through three collinear points?
practice_questions.question_spoken: How many circles can be drawn through three collinear points?
steps: Three collinear points lie on a straight line
A circle is curved — no straight section can be part of a circle
Therefore NO circle can pass through three collinear points
practice_questions.trick: Collinear points → straight line → cannot be on a circle. For a circle, the three points MUST be non-collinear (form a triangle).
practice_questions.alt: A circle is curved everywhere. Three collinear points form a line segment. No circle can contain a straight line section.

practice_questions.question: AB and CD are two equal chords of a circle. Which statement is true?
practice_questions.question_spoken: AB and CD are two equal chords of a circle. Which statement is true about their distances from the centre?
steps: AB = CD → perpendicular distances from centre are equal
Equal chords are always equidistant from the centre
practice_questions.trick: Equal chords ↔ equal distances from centre. This goes both ways. Same length chord = same distance from centre. Always!
practice_questions.alt: The perpendicular from centre to each chord has the same length, because equal chords subtend equal angles and are formed by equal triangles.

practice_questions.question: A, B, C, D are four points on a circle. Angle ABD = 50°. Find angle ACD.
practice_questions.question_spoken: A, B, C, D are on a circle. Angle ABD is 50 degrees. Find angle ACD.
steps: Angles ABD and ACD are in the same segment (both subtend arc AD)
By Theorem 9.9: angles in same segment are equal
practice_questions.trick: Both angles subtend the same arc AD from the same side. Same segment → same angle. No calculation needed!
practice_questions.alt: B and C are both on the same arc. Both see chord AD. By the same-segment theorem, they see it at the same angle: 50°.

practice_questions.question: Prove: a cyclic parallelogram is a rectangle. What is the key step?
practice_questions.question_spoken: To prove a cyclic parallelogram is a rectangle, what is the key step?
practice_questions.trick: Parallelogram gives equal opposite angles. Cyclic gives supplementary opposite angles. Combine: each angle must be 90°!
practice_questions.alt: ∠A = ∠C (parallelogram). ∠A + ∠C = 180° (cyclic). So 2∠A = 180° → ∠A = 90°. Same for ∠B and ∠D. All 90° = rectangle.

practice_questions.question: If arc AB subtends angle AOB = 100° at centre O, what angle does the same arc subtend at point C on the circle?
practice_questions.question_spoken: Arc AB subtends central angle AOB equals 100 degrees. What angle does it subtend at point C on the remaining circle?
practice_questions.trick: Central is always double inscribed. Inscribed is always half central. Divide by 2 every time!
practice_questions.alt: The angle at the centre (100°) is double the angle at any point on the remaining circle. So the inscribed angle = 100÷2 = 50°.
